dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, an unknown problem-person down in the parking lot, suspected opioid overdose.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. Can you explain what happened to the patient?
I don't know his name-he was found unresponsive in the parking lot. He's breathing very slowly.
Checking AVPU. No verbal or physical response to stimulus.
Eyes remain closed with no spontaneous eye opening.
No verbal response-patient does not answer or react.
Nail-bed pressure applied-no motor response beyond minimal flexion.
Slight flexion of the arm suggests a minimal motor response, consistent with GCS 1.
Airway suspected to be compromised by the tongue-performing head-tilt-chin-lift.
Airway open but compromised, shallow breathing at ~4 breaths per minute, carotid pulse strong but unmeasured BP.
Pulse is 100 BPM-strong and regular; carotid palpable.
Breathing shallow and slow, about 4 breaths per minute, effort not labored.
Respiration at 4 breaths per minute-depth shallow and effort minimal.
SpO₂ non-measurable-suggests poor oxygen saturation.
Inserting 26Fr NPA into the right nostril-airway now supported.
BVM connected with 15 LPM of O2-assisting ventilations started.
Circulation seems fair based on strong carotid pulse, skin is warm and moist.
Patient remains unresponsive-AVPU tasteetted as GCS 3.
Attaching 4-lead ECG-assessing for possible dysrhythmia.
EKG initially shows sinus rhythm-no dysrhythmia immediately identified.
Known allergies or medications? Any history?
Allergies include Bactrim. No current medications reported. History of opioid use.
When was the last food or drink? When did the episode start?
Can't confirm last food, drink, or when the episode began.
Partner verifying Narcan 0.1 mg-IV access available for administration.
Administering naloxone IVP-5cc NS flush completed.
Pt is awakening-responsive to voice, but not oriented to time or place.
Pt remains AAOx1/4-oriented to self, not to time or place.
Pulse now measured at 94 BPM-still regular, now consistent with sinus rhythm.
Respiratory rate increased to 19 BPM-adequate depth and effort observed.
SpO₂ improved to 99%-maintained with BVM and O2.
4-lead shows continued sinus rhythm-no new dysrhythmia detected.
IV established in left antecubital fossa with 18G catheter.
Blood glucose is 169 mg/dL-within normal limits.
Narcan confirmed administered-flushing line with saline now.
Pt now alert to self only-confused about time and location.
O2 saturation at 99%-maintained on BVM and high-flow oxygen.
End-tidal CO₂ now at 45mmHg-improved from initial 50mmHg.
Radial pulse strong and regular-no irregularities.
Blood pressure is 128/83 mmHg-improved from 0/0 at baseline.
Pupils pinpoint and equal, estimated at 1-2mm bilaterally.
Skin color pink, warm, and moist-no cyanosis or pallor detected.
Patient has full motion in all extremities with no resistance or pain.
Tympanic temperature 96.8°F-below average, but no signs of hypothermia or fever.
Opioid history confirmed by close friend-this supports overdose suspicion.
Pt denied any drug or alcohol use, but occasionally uses opioids.
Pt denies any pain in any location, upon movement, or chest discomfort.
Pt is not reporting any pain-no exacerbating or relieving factors observed.
We're following protocol 7-1 for opioid-induced respiratory depression and altered mental status.
En route to Richmond Community Hospital-nearest emergency facility.
Pt's pulse is elevated at 113 BPM-indicative of tachycardia.
Now at 11 BPM-down from 19 earlier but still above previously observed baseline.
BP has increased to 141/93 mmHg-mild elevation noted.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia-consistent with pt's current metabolic state.
Pt is breathing independently now-no requirement for continued BVM ventilation.
Latest vitals show normal SpO₂, stable BP and elevated pulse.
Starting five-point seatbelts-preparing to move to stretcher.
Pt is secured on the stretcher with five-point restraints.
Replacing BVM with an EtCO₂ nasal cannula at 4LPM-for ongoing O₂ and ventilation monitoring.
Pt is alert and oriented to self-no confusion observed now.
Sinus tachycardia remains on EKG-patient stable at current rate.
Pt now breathing on own and airway is patent and well maintained.
Continuing under protocol 7-1-opiate overdose with neurological response.
Five-point restraint rechecked and confirmed secure.
All equipment secured-Pt ready for transport to the ED.
ED has been notified-five minutes out, estimated arrival.
Initiating transport now-Pt secured and vitals stable.
27-year-old male presenting with opioid overdose-initially responsive to Narcan.
GCS improved from 3 to 13-14 following IVP naloxone administration.
NPA in place, 18G IV lock active with normal saline.
Patient oxygenation and breathing now fully independent.
